front: Empire: founded by Ismail I; reached its peak under Shah Abbas the Great
Empire: grouped with the Mughal and Ottoman Empires as one of the three "gunpowder empires," a term coined by historian Marshall Hodgson
Empire: established Twelver Shi'a Islam as Iran's state religion
Institution: Sufi order based in Ardabil, militarized first by Sheikh Junayd and then Sheikh Haydar, that developed into the Safavid Empire
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Figure: Safavid Sufi order leader killed fighting the White Sheep (Aq Qoyunlu) Turkomans; his Ardabil mausoleum became a pilgrimage site
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Institution: "red heads," named for a twelve-gored crimson hat ("Haydar's Crown") symbolizing the Twelve Shia Imams, who formed the Safavid Empire's early army
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Battle: decisive victory over the Aq Qoyunlu ("White Sheep") after which Ismail I proclaimed the Safavid Empire
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Ruler: rallied Turkoman Qizilbash forces to take Tabriz, which he made the Safavid capital
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Battle: Ismail I defeated Uzbek leader Muhammad Shaybani here and turned his skull into a jeweled drinking goblet
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Ruler: wrote poetry in Turkic under the pen name "Khata'i," including the Dahnama, and named his children after Shahnameh characters
work: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)

front: Battle: in 1514, Ottoman Sultan Selim the Grim crushed the Safavid Qizilbash cavalry with Janissary musket fire, wounding Ismail I
work: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars

front: Battle: this 1514 battle was the climax of Selim I's retaliation against Safavid support for Shahkulu's Alevi rebellion; the Qizilbash confederation began to splinter afterward
work: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars

front: Treaty: ended the first Ottoman-Safavid war (after Chaldiran), splitting Armenia and Georgia between the two empires
work: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars

front: Battle: fought continuously day and night for a week between the Ottomans and Safavids, hence its name
work: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars

front: Treaty: (also called Qasr-e Shirin) consolidated and extended the Peace of Amasya in the Ottomans' favor, ending a later Ottoman-Safavid war
work: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars

front: Ruler: defeated the Ottomans in 1618, reconquering the Caucasus, Western Iran, Dagestan, and Mesopotamia with ghulam troops
work: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars

front: Institution: drawn from Turkoman tribes like the Shamlu, Rumlu, and Ustajlu, wearing twelve-gored red headgear, motivated by "millenarian" verses proclaiming "I am Alexander" and "I am God's mystery"
work: The Qizilbash: Rise and Fall of the Red Heads

front: Institution: forced Qizilbash members to eat pieces of Murad Beg's body off a skewer to prove loyalty
work: The Qizilbash: Rise and Fall of the Red Heads

front: Figure: cleric who forced the Qizilbash to give up their oral tradition of legends about Abu Muslim
work: The Qizilbash: Rise and Fall of the Red Heads

front: Institution: this group's power diminished after Chaldiran and dissension between its tribes; Shah Abbas the Great curbed and purged them, replacing them in the army with ghulam slave soldiers
work: The Qizilbash: Rise and Fall of the Red Heads

front: Institution: Georgian and Armenian slaves converted to Islam and organized into this force by Abbas I to undermine the Qizilbash's administrative dominance
work: The Qizilbash: Rise and Fall of the Red Heads

front: Ruler: son of Ismail I and the Safavid dynasty's longest-reigning shah; moved the capital to Qazvin
Ruler: signed the Peace of Amasya with the Ottomans and sheltered the fugitive Ottoman Prince Bayezid at his court
Ruler: helped the Mughal emperor Humayun recapture the Mughal throne
Ruler: ordered a massacre of the Takkalu tribe after the Uzbeks invaded Khorasan
Figure: daughter of Tahmasp I who poisoned her sister to install a nearly blind ruler during the Safavid succession crisis, then was strangled by the Qizilbash
Ruler: son of Tahmasp I who tried to restore Sunni Islam in the Safavid Empire and probably died of poison
Ruler: came to power amid Qizilbash assassinations and coups, then restored royal authority and moved the Safavid capital from Qazvin to Isfahan in 1598
work: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan

front: Ruler: built a new tripartite army of topchis, tofangchis, and ghulam slave soldiers with the help of English advisors Robert and Anthony Shirley
work: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan

front: Ruler: retook Hormuz from the Portuguese with English East India Company help, then founded a rival port that caused Hormuz's decline
work: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan

front: Place: port founded by Abbas I after retaking Hormuz from the Portuguese, leading to Hormuz's decline as a trading center
work: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan

front: Event: mass deportation of Armenian merchants and craftsmen under Abbas I, who resettled them in New Julfa near Isfahan, which became a hub of silk production
work: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan

front: Figure: former slave who became a Safavid military leader after helping assassinate a rival named Ostaglu
work: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan

front: Place: vast square in Isfahan overlooked by the Ali Qapu palace, where Safavid rulers watched public events
Place: mosque built by Abbas I on Naqsh-e Jahan Square, noted for its L-shaped entrance and carpet-like tilework listing the Twelve Shia Imams around its mihrab
Work: the Safavid Empire's most famous illustrated manuscript, featuring "The Court of Gayumars" and the "Ship of Shiism"; gifted by a Safavid ruler to Ottoman Sultan Selim II
Work: Safavid-made rugs named for the city of Ardabil; two large examples inscribed with poetry by Hafez are in London's Victoria and Albert Museum
Figure: French jeweler whose Travels recorded Safavid court life, coronation ceremonies, and the silk trade
Figure: Spanish ambassador sent by Philip III who conducted archaeological research at the ruins of Persepolis ("Chilminara") in the Safavid Empire
Figure: recorded two Holstein-Gottorp trade missions to the Safavid Empire meant to stimulate trade with Friedrichstadt
Figure: English brothers who modernized the Safavid army as military advisors
Figure: Circassian noblewoman, "Ambassadress of the King of Persia," who married Robert Shirley and was later painted alongside him by Anthony Van Dyck
Event: Ismail I forcibly converted Persia's population to this branch of Islam to forge a distinct identity against the Sunni Ottomans, creating the world's largest such community
Figure: poet who chronicled the persecution of the Safavid Empire's Jews in "Book of a Forced Convert"
Figure: obtained a fatwa on hajj condemning the Safavid forced-conversion policy and founded the rival Hotak Dynasty in Kandahar
Figure: philosopher who helped the Ishraqi School reach its height of influence during the Safavid dynasty
Work: chronicle of a Safavid ruler's reign written by scribe Iskandar Beg Munshi, beginning with a coup by Murshid Qoli Khan
Event: rebellion that deposed the last Safavid shah, leading to the rise of the Afsharid dynasty from the Afshar clan
Figure: Afsharid conqueror who formally dismantled the Safavid dynasty in 1736
Institution: dynasty that succeeded the Safavids as rulers of Iran
